name: Heme biosynthesis (porphyrin pathway)
summary: Eight-step pathway, shuttling between mitochondrion and cytosol, that builds the iron-protoporphyrin IX cofactor heme from glycine and succinyl-CoA.
description: Heme is the iron-tetrapyrrole prosthetic group of hemoglobin, myoglobin, the respiratory cytochromes, cytochrome c, cytochrome P450s, catalase, peroxidases, and NO synthase. Its synthesis takes eight enzymatic steps that ping-pong between two subcellular compartments. The pathway begins in the mitochondrial matrix, where 5-aminolevulinate synthase (ALAS) condenses glycine with succinyl-CoA (drawn from the citric-acid cycle) in a pyridoxal-5'-phosphate-dependent, decarboxylating reaction to make 5-aminolevulinate (ALA) — the committed, rate-limiting step. ALA is exported to the cytosol, where four soluble enzymes act in series: ALA dehydratase (porphobilinogen synthase, a zinc enzyme) fuses two ALA into the monopyrrole porphobilinogen; hydroxymethylbilane synthase (porphobilinogen deaminase) polymerizes four porphobilinogens into the linear tetrapyrrole hydroxymethylbilane using a covalent dipyrromethane cofactor; uroporphyrinogen-III synthase cyclizes it with inversion of ring D to the physiological III isomer uroporphyrinogen III; and uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase strips the four acetate side chains to methyls, giving coproporphyrinogen III. Coproporphyrinogen III re-enters the mitochondrion, where coproporphyrinogen-III oxidase (in the intermembrane space) oxidatively decarboxylates the ring-A and ring-B propionates to vinyl groups (protoporphyrinogen IX), protoporphyrinogen oxidase (inner membrane) removes six hydrogens to aromatize the macrocycle into protoporphyrin IX, and ferrochelatase inserts ferrous iron to yield protoheme (heme b). Regulation is tissue-specific: the housekeeping isozyme ALAS1 (liver and other tissues) is feedback-inhibited and transcriptionally repressed by heme and induced during fasting via PGC-1alpha (the basis of the carbohydrate/'glucose effect' and of glucose and hemin therapy in acute porphyric attacks), whereas the erythroid isozyme ALAS2 is controlled by iron supply through a 5' iron-responsive element and by erythroid transcription factors, coupling heme output to hemoglobinization. Each of the eight enzymes is tied to a human porphyria when deficient (e.g. acute intermittent porphyria for HMBS, porphyria cutanea tarda for UROD, variegate porphyria for PPOX, erythropoietic protoporphyria for FECH), and the pathway is a classic target of lead toxicity, which inhibits ALA dehydratase and ferrochelatase and causes ALA and zinc-protoporphyrin to accumulate.

sources.id: Lehninger Principles of Biochemistry, 8e, Ch. 22 (biosynthesis of porphyrins and heme)

references.title: Lehninger Principles of Biochemistry, 8th ed. (Ch. 22, porphyrin and heme biosynthesis)
Ajioka RS, Phillips JD, Kushner JP. Biosynthesis of heme in mammals. Biochimica et Biophysica Acta (Molecular Cell Research).
Phillips JD. Heme biosynthesis and the porphyrias. Molecular Genetics and Metabolism.
Puy H, Gouya L, Deybach JC. Porphyrias. The Lancet.

compartments.name: Mitochondrial matrix
compartments.description: Site of the first (ALAS) and last three (CPOX/PPOX/FECH-adjacent) steps; supplies succinyl-CoA from the citric-acid cycle.

compartments.name: Cytosol
compartments.description: Site of the four middle steps (ALAD, HMBS, UROS, UROD), from ALA through coproporphyrinogen III.

compartments.name: Mitochondrial intermembrane space
compartments.description: Location of coproporphyrinogen-III oxidase (CPOX) in humans.

compartments.name: Inner mitochondrial membrane
compartments.description: Houses protoporphyrinogen oxidase (PPOX) and ferrochelatase (FECH); the final steps occur on this membrane, matrix-facing for iron insertion.

metabolites.name: Glycine
metabolites.description: One of the two carbon/nitrogen donors for the pyrrole; provides C2 and the ring nitrogen of ALA.

metabolites.name: Succinyl-CoA
metabolites.description: Citric-acid-cycle-derived C4 dicarboxylic acyl-CoA; its succinyl group is the second precursor condensed by ALAS.

metabolites.name: 5-Aminolevulinate
metabolites.description: First committed intermediate; accumulates in ALAD-deficiency porphyria, acute intermittent porphyria, and lead poisoning and is neurotoxic.

metabolites.name: Porphobilinogen
metabolites.description: The monopyrrole building block; urinary porphobilinogen is markedly elevated during acute porphyric attacks.

metabolites.name: Hydroxymethylbilane
metabolites.description: Unstable linear tetrapyrrole; without UROS it cyclizes non-enzymatically to the non-physiological uroporphyrinogen I isomer.

metabolites.name: Uroporphyrinogen III
metabolites.description: First macrocyclic intermediate; the III isomer (ring D inverted) is the branch point toward heme, siroheme, and (in other organisms) chlorophyll and cobalamin.

metabolites.name: Coproporphyrinogen III
metabolites.description: Tetracarboxylic porphyrinogen (four propionates, four methyls) produced by decarboxylation of the acetate chains; re-enters the mitochondrion.

metabolites.name: Protoporphyrinogen IX
metabolites.description: Two vinyl groups have replaced the ring-A/ring-B propionates; the reduced (porphyrinogen) precursor of protoporphyrin IX.

metabolites.name: Protoporphyrin IX
metabolites.description: Fully aromatic metal-free porphyrin; the direct substrate for iron insertion. Accumulates (with zinc-protoporphyrin) when ferrochelatase is deficient or lead-inhibited.

metabolites.name: Heme b (protoheme IX)
metabolites.description: The pathway product: Fe(II)-protoporphyrin IX. Prosthetic group of hemoglobin, myoglobin, respiratory cytochromes b, cytochrome c (via covalent attachment), catalase and peroxidases; precursor of heme a and heme c.

metabolites.name: Coenzyme A
metabolites.description: Released from succinyl-CoA in the ALAS reaction.

metabolites.name: Ammonia
metabolites.description: Released (as ammonia/ammonium) when porphobilinogen deaminase deaminates each pyrrole during polymerization.

metabolites.name: Dioxygen (O2)
metabolites.description: Terminal electron acceptor for the two mitochondrial oxidase steps (CPOX and PPOX); makes the final steps oxygen-dependent.

metabolites.name: Hydrogen peroxide
metabolites.description: Byproduct of the protoporphyrinogen oxidase reaction (three H2O2 per macrocycle oxidized).

metabolites.name: Ferrous iron (Fe2+)
metabolites.description: The metal inserted into protoporphyrin IX by ferrochelatase; its supply (via the transferrin/mitoferrin route) gates erythroid heme synthesis.

metabolites.name: Pyridoxal 5'-phosphate
metabolites.description: Active form of vitamin B6; the covalently bound cofactor of ALAS that forms a Schiff base with glycine. Vitamin B6 deficiency impairs heme synthesis (sideroblastic anemia).

metabolites.name: Zinc ion (Zn2+)
metabolites.description: Catalytic metal of ALA dehydratase (porphobilinogen synthase); displaced by lead.

metabolites.name: Lead(II) ion (Pb2+)
metabolites.description: Environmental toxicant that inhibits ALA dehydratase (by displacing its catalytic zinc) and ferrochelatase, producing the ALA and zinc-protoporphyrin accumulation diagnostic of lead poisoning.

metabolites.name: D-Glucose
metabolites.description: Nutritional signal: carbohydrate loading suppresses fasting-induced ALAS1 (the 'glucose effect'), used therapeutically to abort acute porphyric attacks.

enzymes.name: 5-Aminolevulinate synthase 1 (non-specific / housekeeping)
enzymes.description: Ubiquitous, heme-regulated isozyme catalyzing the committed, rate-limiting step. Feedback-inhibited and transcriptionally repressed by heme; induced by fasting/drugs via PGC-1alpha. Its dysregulation underlies drug-precipitated acute hepatic porphyric attacks.

enzymes.name: 5-Aminolevulinate synthase 2 (erythroid-specific)
enzymes.description: X-linked erythroid isozyme whose mRNA carries a 5' iron-responsive element, coupling heme output to iron supply. Loss-of-function causes X-linked sideroblastic anemia; gain-of-function (C-terminal) causes X-linked protoporphyria.

enzymes.name: Delta-aminolevulinate dehydratase (porphobilinogen synthase)
enzymes.description: Zinc metalloenzyme that condenses two ALA into porphobilinogen. Strongly inhibited by lead (which displaces the catalytic zinc); rare loss of function causes ALAD-deficiency porphyria (Doss porphyria).

enzymes.name: Hydroxymethylbilane synthase (porphobilinogen deaminase)
enzymes.description: Head-to-tail polymerizes four porphobilinogens on a self-derived covalent dipyrromethane cofactor, releasing ammonia. Heterozygous deficiency causes acute intermittent porphyria (AIP).

enzymes.name: Uroporphyrinogen-III synthase
enzymes.description: Cyclizes hydroxymethylbilane with inversion of ring D to the asymmetric III isomer. Deficiency causes congenital erythropoietic porphyria (CEP, Gunther disease); its absence lets hydroxymethylbilane cyclize non-enzymatically to uroporphyrinogen I.

enzymes.name: Uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase
enzymes.description: Sequentially decarboxylates the four acetate side chains to methyls, converting uroporphyrinogen III to coproporphyrinogen III. Deficiency causes porphyria cutanea tarda (PCT), the most common porphyria; homozygous deficiency causes hepatoerythropoietic porphyria.

enzymes.name: Coproporphyrinogen-III oxidase (oxygen-dependent)
enzymes.description: Oxidatively decarboxylates the ring-A and ring-B propionates to vinyl groups using molecular oxygen. Deficiency causes hereditary coproporphyria (HCP).

enzymes.name: Protoporphyrinogen oxidase
enzymes.description: FAD-dependent six-electron oxidation (three H2O2 released) that aromatizes protoporphyrinogen IX to protoporphyrin IX. Deficiency causes variegate porphyria (VP); the plant ortholog is the target of diphenyl-ether herbicides.

enzymes.name: Ferrochelatase
enzymes.description: Inserts ferrous iron into protoporphyrin IX to complete heme, on the matrix face of the inner membrane. Carries a [2Fe-2S] cluster required for stability; inhibited by lead. Deficiency causes erythropoietic protoporphyria (EPP). EC reclassified from 4.99.1.1 to 4.98.1.1.

genes.name: 5-aminolevulinate synthase 1
genes.description: Encodes the ubiquitous, heme-repressed ALA synthase isozyme; the principal transcriptional control point of hepatic heme synthesis.

genes.name: 5-aminolevulinate synthase 2, erythroid
genes.description: X-linked gene encoding the erythroid ALA synthase; regulated by erythroid transcription factors (e.g. GATA1) and, translationally, by iron via a 5' IRE.

cofactors.role: pyridoxal 5'-phosphate; forms a Schiff base (external aldimine) with glycine

provenance.notes: Committed, rate-limiting step; catalyzed by the housekeeping isozyme ALAS1 or the erythroid isozyme ALAS2. Loss of CO2 makes it effectively irreversible.
Asymmetric Knorr-type condensation of two ALA into the single pyrrole porphobilinogen; the zinc site is the target of lead inhibition.
Sequential head-to-tail assembly of four pyrroles on a covalent dipyrromethane cofactor, deaminating each monomer; yields the unstable linear tetrapyrrole hydroxymethylbilane (preuroporphyrinogen).

reactions.name: Uroporphyrinogen-III synthase — ring closure (ring D inversion)

provenance.notes: Cyclizes the linear tetrapyrrole with inversion of ring D to the physiological type-III isomer; the uncatalyzed reaction instead gives uroporphyrinogen I.

reactions.name: Uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase — acetate to methyl decarboxylation

provenance.notes: Decarboxylates all four acetate side chains to methyl groups in a clockwise sequence starting at ring D.

reactions.name: Coproporphyrinogen-III oxidase — oxidative decarboxylation to vinyls
reactions.equation: Coproporphyrinogen III + O2 -> Protoporphyrinogen IX + 2 CO2 + 2 H2O

provenance.notes: Oxygen-dependent oxidative decarboxylation of the ring-A and ring-B propionate side chains to vinyl groups; occurs after coproporphyrinogen III re-enters the mitochondrion.

reactions.name: Protoporphyrinogen oxidase — aromatization of the macrocycle
reactions.equation: Protoporphyrinogen IX + 3 O2 -> Protoporphyrin IX + 3 H2O2

provenance.notes: FAD-dependent six-electron oxidation removing six hydrogens to form the fully conjugated porphyrin; molecular oxygen is reduced to hydrogen peroxide.

reactions.name: Ferrochelatase — ferrous iron insertion
reactions.equation: Protoporphyrin IX + Fe2+ -> Heme b + 2 H+

provenance.notes: Final step; inserts Fe(II) and displaces the two central pyrrole protons. KEGG R00310 is written in the ferro-lyase (reverse) direction; physiologically it runs toward heme. Rate-limited by iron supply in erythroid cells and inhibited by lead.

regulations.mechanism: End-product feedback: heme directly inhibits ALAS1 activity and blocks import of the ALAS1 precursor from the cytosol into mitochondria, acutely throttling the committed step in hepatocytes when the free-heme pool is adequate.

sources.id: Ajioka, Phillips & Kushner 2006, Biosynthesis of heme in mammals

regulations.mechanism: Heme represses transcription of ALAS1 and destabilizes its mRNA, so a fall in the regulatory heme pool derepresses ALAS1 — the switch that drug- and steroid-induced hepatic heme demand flips during acute porphyric attacks.

The 'glucose effect': fasting induces ALAS1 through the coactivator PGC-1alpha, whereas carbohydrate/glucose intake suppresses PGC-1alpha and thus ALAS1 induction. This is the rationale for intravenous glucose (and hemin) to abort acute hepatic porphyric attacks.

sources.id: Handschin et al., PGC-1alpha regulates ALAS1 and the mammalian heme biosynthetic pathway (Cell 2005)

regulations.mechanism: Translational iron control: the ALAS2 mRNA carries a 5' iron-responsive element (IRE). When iron is replete, iron-regulatory proteins IRP1/2 release the IRE and permit ALAS2 translation, matching erythroid ALA (and therefore heme) synthesis to iron availability; iron scarcity blocks translation.

sources.id: Ajioka, Phillips & Kushner 2006, Biosynthesis of heme in mammals

regulations.mechanism: Lead binds the sulfhydryl-rich active site of ALA dehydratase and displaces its catalytic Zn2+, inhibiting porphobilinogen synthesis; the resulting ALA accumulation (and depressed ALAD activity) is a sensitive marker of lead exposure.

Lead also inhibits ferrochelatase, blocking iron insertion so that protoporphyrin IX accumulates and is chelated with zinc instead; erythrocyte zinc-protoporphyrin is a classic biomarker of chronic lead poisoning and of iron deficiency.

sources.id: Ajioka, Phillips & Kushner 2006, Biosynthesis of heme in mammals

relations.note: ALAS1 encodes the housekeeping 5-aminolevulinate synthase isozyme.
ALAS2 encodes the erythroid-specific 5-aminolevulinate synthase isozyme.
ALAS1 (ubiquitous, heme-repressed) and ALAS2 (erythroid, iron/IRE-controlled) are tissue-specific isozymes catalyzing the identical committed reaction under opposite regulatory logic.
Succinyl-CoA, the C4 precursor condensed by ALAS, is withdrawn from the citric-acid cycle, directly coupling heme output to mitochondrial TCA flux.

The same mitochondrial succinyl-CoA pool is consumed by ketone-body utilization (SCOT/OXCT1 uses succinyl-CoA as the CoA donor to activate acetoacetate), so ketolysis and heme synthesis compete for it.

Heme b is the prosthetic group of the respiratory-chain cytochromes (Complex II heme b, Complex III hemes bL/bH/c1, cytochrome c) and the biosynthetic precursor of the heme a of Complex IV, so heme biosynthesis provisions oxidative phosphorylation.
